data.name: Chainat Bird Park
data.introduction: This is located 4 kilometres before arriving in Chainat. Birds of more than 100 species live in a huge cage which maintains a natural environment. The park also has an aquarium which has a collection of various kinds of freshwater fish found in the Chao Phraya River. Local products such as bird models, wickerwork, and pomelo is available at the shops in the park.

data.name: Hat Yong Ling
data.introduction: Situated on the way to Chao Mai Beach, Hat Yong Ling and Hat San are curved beaches that run parallel to a pine forest. At the end of the beach is a high mountain with niches, caves and a passageway to a cliff with more caves and rocks, similar to a fortress encircling sand dunes. The beach is ideal for swimming and relaxing. The park headquarters and a tourist service center provide services to tourists.

data.name: Wat Mani Chonlakhan
data.introduction: Wat Mani Chonlakhan stands as an ancient and prominent temple in the Phrommat Subdistrict, serving as a symbolic gateway to Lop Buri. Assumably built during King Narai the Great's reign, it was initially named Wat Kao Kaeo due to its location amidst the Lop Buri River. However, with the construction of a road passing through the temple, its island-like appearance was altered. His Majesty King Rama V the Great bestowed upon it the new name "Wat Mani Chonlakhan."

data.name: Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat
data.introduction:
Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat stands across from the Lop Buri railway station, its origins shrouded in mystery. Upon stepping onto the temple grounds, visitors are greeted by the 
“Sala Plueang Khrueang” pavilion, a place for a king to get changed before attending the religious ceremony in the wihan or ubosot. Now, only its pillars remain, hinting at its former grandeur. Adjacent to the pavilion is the“Wihan Luang,” a monumental structure commissioned during the reign of King Narai the Great. Adorned with a Thai-style rectangular main entrance and French Gothic-inspired pierced windows, the wihan enshrines a Buddha image on a masonry base known as Chukkachi. It was a collaborative effort between Lop Buri province and devout Buddhists.

data.name: Phra Prang Sam Yot
data.introduction: Phra Prang Sam Yot is located on a mound on the west side of the railway near San Phra Kan. The compound comprises 3 prangs linked to one another by a corridor. It reflects the Bayon style of Khmer art Dating back roughly to the 12th-13th century. The sanctuaries are crafted from a laterite, and adorned with intricate stucco reliefs. The colonettes were engraved into a hermit, sitting with raised knees under the Ruean Kaeo shelter, which was typical of the Bayon style in Khmer art. Initially serving as a pedestal for enshrining a Buddha image, the central sanctuary boasts a stunning wooden ceiling, its red asterisks patterns a captivating sight.

data.name: Phra Narai Ratchaniwet
data.introduction: Situated within Tha Hin Subdistrict, Phra Narai Ratchaniwet’s construction, favoured by King Narai the Great in 1666, aimed to establish a regal abode in Lop Buri. His Majesty habitually dwelled within its walls for 8-9 months annually.

data.name: Bhumibol Dam (Yanhi Dam)
data.introduction: Thailand’s first and tallest curved concrete dam, standing 154 metres high at its crest. Built across the Ping River at Khao Kaeo, Sam Ngao District, the dam serves for hydroelectric power generation and irrigation. The Ping River flows from the dam to Hot District, Chiang Mai Province, over a distance of 207 kilometres. The dam’s surroundings offer recreational and fish breeding areas.

data.name: Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary
data.introduction: A conservation forest established in 1989 to preserve natural resources, providing a habitat for various wildlife species. The region predominantly features complex high mountains with evergreen forest and deciduous forest.

data.name: King Taksin the Great National Park
data.introduction: Spanning 149 square kilometres, encompasses the Mae Tho forest in Mae Tho and Phawo Subdistricts, Mueang Tak District, and the Mae Lamao forest in Mae Sot District. Its terrain features steep and complex mountains, with ecosystems including hill evergreen forest, pine forest, evergreen forest, dry dipterocarp forest, and mixed deciduous forest. The cool climate throughout the year supports diverse wildlife. Declared as Thailand's 40th national park, it is a significant conservation area.

data.name: Si Thep Historical Park
data.introduction: The Ancient Town of Si Thep is a significant historical site that was recently designated as Thailand’s fourth UNESCO World Cultural Heritage site. This recognition was granted during the 45th session of the World Heritage Committee on September 19, 2023, in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. The site was officially inscribed under the name "The Ancient Town of Si Thep and its Associated Dvaravati Monuments."

data.name: Doi Soi Malai Petrified Wood National Park
data.introduction: The Park is located in Tak Ok Subdistrict, within the national forest reserve area of Mae Salit-Pong Daeng. The park covers approximately 355.42 square kilometres and is predominantly dense with Deciduous Dipterocarp Forests. The Department of National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation has discovered several petrified wood logs throughout the area and conducted excavations in 7 pits between 2003-2005, unearthing 8 large petrified wood logs, leading to the development of the site as a tourist attraction and a natural learning site for Tak Province.

data.name: Pa Charoen Waterfall National Park
data.introduction: This park spans approximately 855 square kilometres, covering Phop Phra and Mae Sot Districts. The park's terrain is predominantly mountainous with a few plains, featuring notable peaks; such as, Doi Pa Tan, Doi Den Krating, Doi Luang, Doi Mak Muen, and Khao Lam Pak Yen, which are part of the Thanon Thongchai Range. These mountains are the sources of Mae Lamau Stream, Um Piem Stream, and others, with the area hosting mixed deciduous and pine forests and naturally occurring Dok Kra Jaeo flowers (Curcuma sessilis).

data.name: Mae Moei National Park
data.introduction: Located within Mae Usu and Mae Song Subdistricts, the park spans an area of 185.28 square kilometres. The park's terrain features complex mountain ranges with lush forests and diverse wildlife, including dry evergreen forest, tropical rain forest, hill evergreen forest, and mixed deciduous forest. It is a habitat for various wildlife and an important source for the Mae Song, Mae Usu, Mae Salit Luang, and Moei Rivers. The park was officially established on 20 May 1999. The park headquarters area is landscaped and decorated with flowering and ornamental plants, offering a tranquil atmosphere amidst the mountainous terrain.

data.name: Wat Tha It
data.introduction: Located in Bang Phlap Subdistrict, it stands as a symbol of spiritual resilience. The temple originated from a modest brick kiln, established in 1761 to produce bricks for the construction of Wat Khun Inthapramun. Now stands as a sanctuary of faith, echoing whispers of bygone eras. Here, stands statues of Luang Pho Phet and Luang Pho Khao, crafted in Ayutthaya's golden age, with the latter, once enshrined in wihan “Maha-Ut”.

data.name: Wat Ban Phran
data.introduction: Located in Sri Phran Subdistrict, its story echoes the legendary tales of Luang Pu Chai Mongkhon, a revered monk of Ayutthaya Province. Legend has it that the temple was constructed by a couple called Mr. Phan and Mrs. Ngoen, and their son, Mr. Krapuk Thong, at the end of Sukhothai era during 1319-1327. Subsequently abandoned, the temple flooded in dense forest for over a century until hunters established a village nearby and began restoring it.

data.name: Nong Chet Sen Monkey Cheek Development Project, Royal Initiative
data.introduction: The endeavour to develop the Nong Chet Sen, Monkey Cheek water retention area, inspired by royal initiative, is situated in Hua Phai Subdistrict, adjacent to the Asia Road heading to the city of Ang Thong. Spanning across a vast expanse of 539,200 square metres, this project is a tribute to the royal benevolence of King Rama IX, bestowed to alleviate natural disasters and provide water for consumption, agriculture, and animal husbandry, while nurturing aquatic life and providing habitat for various bird species. It stands as a commemoration of the auspicious occasion of His Majesty's 84th Birthday Anniversary.

data.name: Wat Pa Mok Worawihan
data.introduction: Wat Pa Mok Worawihan is situated within the Pa Mok District, along the western banks of the Chao Phraya River. Within its sacred confines resides an exquisite reclining Buddha image, crafted from golden-clad bricks and plaster, stretching an awe-inspiring 22.58 metres from the sole of its feet to the tip of its crown. Legend has it that this statue once floated adrift upon the river's currents, before fate, guided by the benevolent hands of devotees, raised it ashore to be enshrined along the riverbank.

Once a bastion of governance, this building has been transformed into a sanctuary of history, paying homage to the regal traditions that have shaped the nation. Among its hallowed halls, visitors embark on a journey through time, exploring the rich tapestry of royal coronation ceremonies that have defined Thai culture for centuries.

data.introduction: It spans 6,427.34 square kilometres across 3 provinces; Uthai Thani, Kanchanaburi, and Tak. data.name: Tham Prathun Non-Hunting Area
data.introduction: The area is in Pa O Subdistrict. With an area of 20.88 square kilometres, it was declared a non-hunting area on 4 March 1999. The topography includes limestone mountains, various species of plants, with dense Tat trees.

data.name: Tham Khao Wong Forest Park
data.introduction: Located in Ban Rai Subdistrict, Tham Khao Wong Forest Park is part of the western forest bordering the Phu Toei National Park in Suphan Buri province. The area is characterized by limestone mountains with steep slopes, serving as a vital watershed for streams that traverse Ban Rai District.

data.name: Wat Tham Khao Wong
data.introduction: Wat Tham Khao Wong, located in Ban Rai Subdistrict, sits along the mountainous terrain. The temple is a four-story traditional Thai wooden structure constructed from teak and Makha wood, including old wood removed from Thai houses in Ayutthaya and Ang Thong provinces.

data.name: Wat Uposatharam
data.introduction: Wat Upothisataram, situated in Thepho Subdistrict within the municipality of Uthai Thani, holds a rich history dating back to the early Rattanakosin era. Originally named Wat Bot Manorom or Wat Bot nestled along the banks of the Sakae Krang River.

Wat Phra Borommathat
data.introduction: Situated in Ban Pak Khlong, Koei Chai Subdistrict, is a temple of significant historical and cultural value, located at the confluence of the Yom and Nan Rivers. Originally known as Wat Borommathat, due to its stupa (Phra Borommathat Chedi) containing the relics of the Buddha on an octagonal base, the temple is believed to have been built during the reign of Somdet Phra Sanphet VIII (Phra Chao Suea) of Ayutthaya.

data.name: King Taksin the Great Shrine
Wat Nong Pho
data.introduction: Located in Ban Nong Pho, Nong Pho Subdistrict, this temple is rich with historical background, initially established in the Ayutthaya period but later abandoned. It was restored in 1931, and its name has evolved over time due to its unique natural surroundings. Originally called "Wat Somphot Pho Krachai" and later "Wat Somphot Pho Yen,". Eventually, the temple's name was changed to "Wat Nong Pho" by Somdet Phra Maha Samana Chao Krom Phraya Vajirananavarorasa during his official visit, aligning it with the name of the village.

data.name: Tham Phet-Tham Thong Forest Park
data.introduction: Nestled between Khao Chon Duea and Khao Khwang in the Takhli and Tak Fa Districts, Tham Phet-Tham Thong Forest Park is a splendid natural sanctuary. Situated at the terminus of the Phetchabun mountain range on the right bank of the Pa Sak River, it boasts a diverse topography comprising small limestone mountain ranges, mixed deciduous forest, steep limestone hills, and surrounding farmlands. This scenic forest park was officially established by the Royal Forest Department on 10 January 1997.

data.name: Nagaraja Shrine
data.introduction: Situated in the Chan Sen Subdistrict, the shrine stands as a cherished and sacred site within the community. Its significance is rooted in the belief that Chao Pho Nagaraja, or the King Serpent, guardian of underground treasures, resides here.

data.name: Wat Saket and the Golden Mount
data.introduction: This landmark, on the only hill in Bangkok, is of great significance for all followers of The Lord Buddha. 320 stairs take you up to the top for panoramic views of the Rattanakosin Island.

Chao Ka Market
data.introduction: Located in the Khao Din Subdistrict along the scenic Ping River, the market offers a nostalgic glimpse into traditional Thai culture. Positioned in front of Wat Phra Nor Tharinthorn and near Wat Warin Kongkaram (Wat Khao Din Tai), this community market is renowned for its distinctive retro ambience. A unique aspect of Chao Ka Market is the vendors, who don traditional Thai costumes and conclude their conversations with “Chao Kha,” a respectful and traditional Thai expression.

data.name: Wat Si Uthumphon
data.introduction: Also known as Wat Luang Pho Choi, located in the Nong Krot Subdistrict, is a temple with a rich history and unique architectural features. Established in 1942 by Phra Kru Choi Chanthasuwanno, a renowned figure in Pak Nam Pho and affectionately known as the god of Pak Nam Pho, he served as the abbot until his passing. The temple was initially named Wat Wang Dua.

data.name: Phasan Building (The Origin of the Chao Phraya River)
data.introduction: Located in Ko Yom, which is the origin of the Chao Phraya River, the building is a new Landmark in Nakhon Sawan. This site is not only a tourist attraction but also a learning centre of the history and culture. The word “Phasan” is derived from the word “Phasan” which means merging. In this context “Phasan” has the exclusive meaning, that is, guiding people to unite and create a blending of people, places, and time.

Phra Tamnak Doi Tung
data.introduction: Phra Tamnak Doi Tung, formerly the residence where HRH Princess Srinagarindra (Somdet Ya) conducted her royal duties, showcases a fusion of Lanna architecture and Swiss chalet design. Its intricate wooden carvings, gabled roofs, and diverse window patterns, skillfully crafted by northern artisans, create a captivating ambiance. Surrounding the residence, a lush flower garden awaits, complemented by audio guides available in Thai and English for visitors to explore various points of interest.

data.name: Phrathat Chedi Srinagarindra Sathit Maha Santikhiri
data.introduction: Situated atop Doi Mae Salong in Mae Salong Nok Subdistrict, Phrathat Chedi Srinagarindra Sathit Maha Santikhiri stands 1,500 metres above sea level, just 4 kilometres north of Ban Santikhiri. Completed in 1996, it was constructed to honor HRH Princess Srinagarindra (Somdet Ya).

data.name: Doi Mae Salong
data.introduction: Doi Mae Salong, the location of Ban Santikhiri, formerly known as Mae Salong Nok, was once a settlement for former soldiers of the 93rd Chinese Division who migrated from the Republic of the Union of Myanmar. Spanning from December to February, the cool winter air brings forth the blooming of wild Himalayan cherry (Prunus cerasoides), the smallest variety of Sakura, with delicate pink and white blossoms adorning the path up Doi Mae Salong.

Wat Pa Sak
data.introduction: Wat Pa Sak, situated in Wiang Subdistrict, stands as a significant ancient site in Chiang Saen. Renowned for its exquisite stucco craftsmanship dating back to the early Lanna period, it holds a prominent place in the region's historical heritage. According to chronicles, this temple was erected during the reign of King Saen Phu, the third monarch of the Mengrai dynasty. Its purpose was to enshrine the relics of the Lord Buddha’s right medial malleolus. King Saen Phu commissioned the planting of teak trees extensively across the temple grounds, totaling up to 300 trees, which subsequently led to the temple being named as such.

data.name: Ban Tha Khanthong Community
Wat Phrathat Pha Ngao
data.introduction: Wat Phrathat Pha Ngao, situated in Ban Sop Kham, Wiang Subdistrict, holds significant historical importance from the Yonok Kingdom era. Originally known as Wat Sop Kham and nestled along the banks of the Mekong River, the temple faced substantial destruction due to flooding. Consequently, in 1976, a new temple was erected on the site. During the temple's reconstruction, several ancient Buddha images, dating back 700 to 1,300 years, were unearthed. Among these treasures was the revered "Luang Pho Pha Ngao" Buddha image, named after the grand shadow cast by a rock formation resembling a stupa when illuminated by sunlight.

data.name: Phrathat Chedi Luang
data.introduction: Phrathat Chedi Luang is situated in Wiang Subdistrict, adjacent to the Chiang Saen National Museum. It is the oldest and largest temple in Chiang Saen, built during the 19th Buddhist century by Phrachao Saen Phu, the royal descendant of King Mengrai, the founder of the Lanna Kingdom. The purpose was to enshrine the Lord Buddha’s relics. Subsequently, Phra Mueang Kaeo ordered the removal of the original chedi's base and the construction of a new one in 1515.

data.name: Phu Soi Dao National Park

The park is known for its well-preserved natural forests and as a source of numerous streams. The highest peak of Phu Soi Dao is 2,102 metres above sea level, which is the 4th highest in Thailand.

data.name: Sirikit Dam
data.introduction: Thailand's largest earthen dam is a significant structure built as part of the Nan River Development Project. Initially named "Pha Som Dam," it was later renamed in honour of Queen Sirikit following royal permission. The dam stands as a testament to Thailand's advancement in water resource management and engineering.

With an area of approximately 999 square kilometres, it features a complex mountainous landscape encompassing diverse and pristine forests. The park also serves as a vital watershed of the reservoir above the Sirikit Dam.

data.introduction: Situated in Ban Na Lup Laeng, Pa Khai Subdistrict amidst the stunning natural beauty of Thong Saen Khan District, this temple is a branch of Wat Na Luang (Abhinya Tesit Dhamma) and stands as a remarkable destination for spiritual tourism in Uttaradit Province. The temple is renowned for its exquisite art and tranquil surroundings, offering visitors a serene environment for reflection and worship.

data.name: Bo Lek Nam Phi Museum
data.introduction: Located in Ban Nam Phi, Nam Phi Subdistrict, the museum is a significant site dedicated to the history of high-grade steel called Lek Nam Phi. The museum exhibits the complete procedures of making high-grade hand-forged Lek Nam Phi swords, which were the weapons of ancient Thai warriors.

data.name: Guan Yin Temple (Nam Rit)
data.introduction: Located in Ban Chai Khao, Nam Rit Subdistrict, the temple began as a meditation centre dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guan Yin. It became the Buddhabhavanadhammasathan Foundation (Guan Yin Temple, Ban Chai Khao), serving as a Dhamma practice centre. The temple not only aids the poor and underprivileged but also plays a vital role in promoting and preserving Thai-Chinese arts and culture.

data.name: Ban Kanokmani Museum
data.introduction: Located on Borom At Road in Tha It Subdistrict, Ban Kanokmani Museum is an architecturally significant building constructed in 1933. It was established by Ammat Tho (Lieutenant Colonel) Phraya Athayasaiwisut (Chot Kanokmani), a former governor of Uttaradit Province (1931 - 1933) and the progenitor of the Kanokmani family. After his retirement, he and his family chose to settle in Uttaradit.

data.name: Wat Tha Tanon
data.introduction: Located along the bank Nan River on Kaset Rat Road in Tha It Subdistrict and just opposite the Uttaradit Railway Station. This historic temple, originally known as "Wat Wang Tao Mo," houses the provincial Buddha image of Luang Pho Phet, a Chiang Saen-style Buddha image cast in bronze in the so-called diamond posture.

The course has now expanded to 36 holes to accommodate the increasing number of golfers and create a challenge quite different from the first 27 holes. data.name: Wat Traimit Withayaram
data.introduction: Phra Phutthathatsaphonyan, the principal Buddha image in the ordination hall, is a Buddha image in the Mara-Vijaya attitude, made of stucco and lacquered with gold leaf. The general public calls it “Luang Pho To” or “Luang Pho Wat Sam Chin”. People often come to make offerings of jasmine garlands. King Chulalongkorn once traveled to pay homage and praised it as a Buddha image with extremely beautiful features. “Luang Pho Mo”, the former abbot of Wat Traimit Witthayaram, once made amulets to distribute. He made a figure of Phra Phutthathatsaphonyan made of lead and called it Luang Pho To Wat Sam Chin. It turned out that it was popular among the general public as being very sacred. It is now rare to find.

data.name: Rajjaprabha Dam Reservoir (Chiao Lan)
data.introduction: This clay and stone dam, located in Khao Phang sub-district, is 95 metres high and 700 metres long with a 100-metre-high wall, and features over 100 small islands spread across its vast reservoir. The dam is the south’s second multi-purpose dam and reservoir and doubles as a hydro-electric power station, as well as having agricultural and fishing purposes. Visitors can take a leisurely boat trip around the reservoir to enjoy some great views with the striking limestone peaks in the background and understand why the area is known as “Thailand’s Guilin” One of the most interesting points around the dam and reservoir is ‘Khao Sam Gler’ peak. Affectionately known as the ‘three friends’ by locals, this trio of limestone peaks emerges from the reservoir in a distinctive fashion and is surrounded by incredible views of the surrounding greenery and emerald waters. The Pakarang Cave is located in the 500 Rai inlet and is maintained under the jurisdiction of the Forest Protection Unit, Khao Sok National Park. The name translates as ‘cave of corals’ and is so named due to the stalagmites and stalactites formed from eroded limestone which resemble an underwater coral reef. There have also been numerous fossils unearthed in the area, which have been dated back to a period between 250 and 400 million years ago.

data.name: Thap Lan National Park
data.introduction:
Covering areas in Wang Nam Khiao, Pak Thong Chai, Khon Buri, and Soeng Sang Districts in Nakhon Ratchasima Province and
Na Di District in Prachin Buri Province, Thap Lan National Park spans an impressive 2,245.88 square kilometres. It is the second-largest national park in Thailand, following Kaeng Krachan National Park, which covers 3,034.42 square kilometres. A highlight of Thap Lan National Park is its vast dry dipterocarp forest, the last of its kind in Thailand, which remains in a pristine condition. This forest serves as a vital source of several important rivers, including the Bang Pakong and Mun rivers, supporting the livelihood of people in the Northeastern and Eastern regions of Thailand.

data.name: Farm Chokchai
data.introduction:
Located along Highway No. 2 (Mittraphap Road) towards Bangkok, Farm Chokchai is not only one of the largest dairy farms in Asia
but also a popular agritourism destination. It won the Thailand Tourism Award from the Tourism Authority of Thailand four onsecutive
times in 2006, 2008, 2010, and 2012, leading to its induction into the Hall of Fame. The farm is recognized for its systematic management, earning the distinction of being Thailand’s first standard dairy farm.

data.name: The Old Building of Ban Singha Tha
Phuket Old Town
data.introduction: Comprising of the Thanon Klang, Thanon Dibuk, Thanon Phang Nga, Thanon Yaowarat, Thanon Krabi and Soi Romanee roads, the area is steeped in history and in bygone days was inhabited by settlers from many different nations and ethnic groups, including Chinese, Indians, Europeans and Muslims who came to trade or put down their roots. These early settlers built terraced homes in a style that later became known as ‘Sino-European’ architecture. Way back in the reign of King Rama IV, the tin mining industry flourished but these days the mines are preserved as museums and many local buildings have been converted into accommodation, dining spaces, coffee shops and souvenir shops. There is also a large market and walking street which is organized every Sunday between 16.00-22.00 hrs. along Thalang Road to give visitors the chance to sample the multicultural charms of the old town.

data.name: Ko Lat I-Tan Community
data.introduction: Located in the Sam Phran District, Ko Lat I-Tan is a flat, pig's stomach-shaped area formed by canal excavation. The canal's estuary lies between Song Khanong and Hom Kret Subdistrict, and flows across the Tha Chin River, creating the island-like Ko Lat I-Tan. This region comprises three subdistricts: Rai Khing, Bang Toei, and Song Khanong. The area boasts fertile land and abundant water sources, ideal for fruit orchards, particularly for the renowned pomelo varieties such as Khao Phong, Khao Paen, Khao Thong Di, Khao Nam Phueng, and Khao Hom.

data.name: Wat Ratchabophit Sathitmahasimaram
data.introduction: The temple is located on Fuang Nakhon Road near Wat Pho. Built by King Rama V in 1869, it was in keeping with tradition that each monarch constructed a temple to mark his reign. The temple is a mixture of local and western styles, showing an awakening interest in new ideas and a desire to experiment with them. The exterior of the chapel is in the Thai style, but the interior is decorated in the European style.

data.name: Krom Luang Chumphon Khet Udomsak Shrine
data.introduction: The Shrine is a significant historical and cultural site located on a hill at the northern end of Sai Ri Beach, Sai Ri Subdistrict. This memorial honours Admiral Prince Krom Luang Chumphon Khet Udomsak, affectionately known as “Sadet Tia,” revered as the Father of the Thai Navy. In addition to his military role, His Royal Highness also earnestly studied traditional medicine and was proficient in treating various illnesses. He offered medical care to patients under the moniker “Mo Phon or Doctor Phon”, free of charge.

data.introduction: This cultural tourism community showcases the colourful, multicultural lifestyles of the Muslims, Buddhists, Thais and Chinese descendants who coinhabit the village. As far as activities go, there’s the ‘Test Your WIllingness’ bridge to cross, boat trips to view the local Brahminy Kites circling for prey, diving for fat innkeeper worms and lamp shell clam, exploring the mangrove forests and studying the local lifestyles and culture with its mix of two religions and three ethnic origins. Visitors can also enjoy demonstrations of hat making from nipa palm leaves and crispy local snacks known as ‘Tang-me’ and ‘Khao Krieb Ya Na’ crackers.

data.name: Thai Red Cross Society, Khao Lan
Ko Khai Huaro (Laughing Island)
data.introduction: Tucked away behind Ko Kradat, this minuscule island features a single tree jutting out above the water, resembling the image often featured in the popular Thai cartoon strip ‘Khai Huaro’, hence the island’s name. Ko Khai Huaro can be reached from Ko Kradat at low tide only. Tourists can purchase a tour package from any of the travel agents or tour companies on Ko Mak for around 700 baht/person (including transport to and from the resort). It takes around 10 minutes to reach the Ko Kradat in a 7 seater boat and the tour takes visitors to see the deer and coconut plantations on the island before stopping at King Rama V cape and allowing them to walk across to Ko Khai Huaro while the tide is at its lowest ebb.

data.introduction:
Ban Thammachat Lang lies in Khlong Yai subdistrict, with land corals, 360-degree panoramic views and a range of diverse activities to take part in, including tie-dying fabric at Sam Pa, a local centre for organic nano-fertilizer and exploring the nature trail set up to bring in extra income and create sustainability for the local people. 
Visitors can learn all about the fertilizer production process from community members, watch the local fisherman in action as they fish for prawns, mussels and clams, crabs and fish, as well as watching the sunset over Ko Chang Island and spending the night in a beachfront homestay.

Ko Mak
data.introduction: Lying between Ko Chang and Ko Kut, 38 km. from the coastline, Ko Mak covers just 14 square km. and resembles a four-pointed star when viewed from above. Most of the fringes of the island are kept cool and shady by rows of coconut and rubber trees and the beaches are clean and golden with clear waters, especially around Ao Tanit, Ao Pai, Ao Prong, Ao Phat and Ao Daeng. Most of the waters around Ko Mak also boast healthy, colourful coral reefs. Getting there: Tourists can take ferries from Krom Luang pier, Laem Ngop and five boats are running to a seasonal timetable.

data.name: Budo-Su-ngai Padi National Park (Pacho Waterfall)
data.introduction: Covers some parts of Narathiwat, Yala, and Pattani Provinces and has an area of 84,239 acres. The Khao Budo mountain range is part of the Indo-Malayan tropical rainforest. Tropical evergreen forests have high humidity because it rains all-year round. It is also the most biodiverse forest compared to other forests in the same area.

data.name: Phra Ram Ratchaniwet
data.introduction: Originally called "Wang Ban Pun", the European style palace is situated to the south of the town. The construction began in 1909 at the command of King Rama V to be his rainy season palace. A German architect, Mr. Karl Deurring designed the palace, while Admiral HRH Prince Boriphat of Nakhonsawan and HRH Prince Damrong supervised the construction. The construction was completed in 1916 in the reign of King Rama VI. The place was used to welcome and accommodate state visitors during the reign of King Rama VI.
